contents:
Here’s a list of resources used for the theme used on this site, Unrealised. It’s a custom hugo theme by me (mwahaha!). I feel like this theme still has a lot of unrealised potential, so the theme is still a work in progress. This list will be updated to reflect any new resources / sources of inspiration.
Inspiration  Theme name inspiration: Unrealised Reality, by Kari Sigurdsson  Resources  Base theme (theme that taught me to make this theme): kimcc’s Noteworthy theme Dev stack icons: Dev Icon
title: List of resources used for this theme (Unrealised)

contents:
Finally got round to making a new theme for this website. I figured it was well-needed to learn the ins and outs of Hugo a bit better.
Over the next few days, I plan to:
 Add the (planned) missing features. Refine the theme. Make the theme live. Update the projects/CV sections. Publish the new content as well.  The plan is to update the theme yearly to keep up to date with latest best practices. But whatever, that can wait for another day.
title: New theme for the website

contents:

They all say don’t need a mend

All spoiled for sheppas' fun

contents:
Background Based on a game called Host no Abunai Sekai (ホストのアブナイ世界), which roughly translates to “The Dangerous World of Hosts”. I didn’t understand a word of it, but it was surprisingly fun. Yes, even if you didn’t understand a word of it. The story starts with the player (you) getting scammed by a loan shark. Then you start running a host club where you scam other women in order to pay back that loan. There are a number of upgrades that would help you earn more money over time. The expressions of the ones who got scammed by you were absolutely priceless.
But the game wasn’t really catered for long-term playing and wasn’t hardcore enough. My game will be a bit more hardcore than that. The loan you have will now have a 50% interest rate per week (I plan to give all crippling depression as you give away your money every week). There’ll be a more intelligent customer and employee host AI which you may benefit from, if you don’t fall into the traps. Maybe they’ll be a 98% success cap, just to mess with you.
This game isn’t the most ethical (well, scamming isn’t ethical but you aren’t killing I guess?), but it’s fun. Hopefully anyway.
Objectives  Replicate the basic mechanics of Host no Abunai Sekai. Add mechanics to increase its difficulty and nastiness.  Approach  Game Engine: Godot. Simple, light, great for 2D games and my 4GB RAM slug of a laptop can handle it. Deployment: Offline mobile game. Autosaves to local cache.  Main Tasks  Make the art Code the mechanics Deploy  Results Still in a work in progress. I will most likely post some updates in my blog. Beta testing will be most likely up by next year.
title: Host Club

contents:
I’ve been using a Macbook Air Mid-2013 from, well, 2013. It was my first high quality laptop, and one that was the state of art at the time. From the moment I got this laptop, I thought, I’m now married to my laptop.
But as I grow up, I realise that…maybe I’ve just grown out of it. Maybe it’s because 4 GB RAM doesn’t do much apart from browsing the web. Maybe it’s because I want to run stuff like Blender and my laptop just can’t handle it. Maybe because so many programs run on Windows and this laptop runs them like a slug.
As long as I compromise, I can still get away with running Godot or an art program or two. Got to cut the music in the background though, too taxing even if I use Firefox over Chrome.
Well, it’s hard to say whether I love or hate my laptop.
I think I’m still leaning towards like, but it’s almost time to get a new laptop.
I can’t decide whether to go for a beefy business laptop or a gaming laptop. Gonna go straight for 16+ GB of RAM and maybe with an upgradeable SSD. A dedicated graphics card would be amazing, and the new AMD Ryzens look pretty great.
Unfortunately, with Covid around, the underspec’d feels overpriced and a lot of the somewhat more reasonably better-spec’d devices are out of stock. It doesn’t help that in New Zealand, there aren’t that many options to choose from as well.
Oh well, I might just have to compromise with using my laptop for another two years or so. Hopefully I’ll replace it in 2023.
title: Thoughts on replacing my laptop

contents: Too much you want to do and not enough time. Too much on your plate and not enough what you want. Such a paiiiiin….
title: Sometimes there's just too much stuff

contents:
Sheppas scrabble day and night,
Diving right in with delight.
Cuby gives sheppas a frown -
Time for sheppas to go down.
All gifted back as slaves,
Sheppas sigh and carve their graves.
Beache smirks with boastful glee,
Watching sheppas scram and flee.
But this time Beache “plays fair”,
Giving sheppas work to share:
As long as all work is done,
Extra time is left for fun.
Sadly there are selfish punks,
Lazing about, being drunks.
These sheppas are the victims,
Of the Beache’s fearsome whims….
But there are the tragic few,
Bullied as a thing to chew,
No particular reason,
Just for some made up treason.

contents:
Sheppas forced to work all day
For Beache with no sick pay
Against Beache they riot
Beache knocks them out one shot
More sheppas rise in their place
Stopping Beache is a race
Beache hits them with a smirk
Tossing them with a knee jerk
They slowly start to stack up
Can’t seem em unless closeup
Sheppas pile nice and high
Beache prices her supply
Cuby frowns at the carpet
When was it a flea market
All those sheppas to be sold
With cost discounted tenfold
Then she sees a zombie rise
Or not, she thought in surprise
But it seemed to have no soul
Charging into Beache coal
Beache knocks it back again
Does it every now and then
Be my slave or continue
Makes no difference in my view
Cuby joins the sheppa side
Charging in with a free ride
The sheppa is knocked away
Nothing is in Cuby’s way
Beache catches the Cuby
Sneering at sheppas in glee
Beache says she wants to sleep
Sheppas are now too dirt cheap
Cuby takes over the throne
Carrying evil on loan
Yet Cuby is far too nice
Makes a sheppa paradise
title: Selling sheppas (2)

contents:

Opens a store, selling chop.
Where do parts originate?
From sheppas out on a date.
It becomes the new food trend,
Sheppas' spirits start to bend.
They serve Beache head to toe,

Beache sits back on her chair,
Sitting with a queenly air.
Sheppas pool around her feet,
Sighing/crying in defeat.
At the store of sheppa chop,
Slowly sales start to drop,
Closure becomes imminent,
Sheppa cheer is evident.

contents:
Hey, I’m Aileen Huang. My main job title(s) don’t include being a web developer, but I do do a lot of it in work and my spare time.
For work, it ends up being client reporting portals, internal tools, Wordpress stuff or vanilla JS snippets. On the side, i.e. my spare time, I like to write private tools that can speed up my job (as per job title), static site generation or mess around with Godot (game engine).
I kinda like to work (min 46 hours per week), but I have a slightly potty mouth that complains about work a lot. My mouth is pottier under stress - so much that my supervisor calls me a grumpy cat - but then my colleagues discuss their ideas to help me so it ends up being alright.
I think, I kinda like to code. But then I’m kinda lazy, so I don’t really know. And I’m not that good at it, which is discouraging. I’m better at coding than I am at math though, that’s for sure.
Apart from coding, I like to do the occasional travelling, doodling, listening to music, writing awful rhyming poems, reading survival horror manga & graphic novels. Not a fan of jump scares in horror movies though. But I’ll probably just tell you, my hobbies are sleeping, eating and breathing.
About this site! I guess this site is mainly for me to unleash my inner potential grumbles. But to make the site sound a bit fancier, let’s synonymise grumbles to quibbles.
Credits Credits to: Photo by Federico Di Dio photography on Unsplash
Final comments I think I’m pretty unlikeable judging by how many “I"s there are in this page, but that’s kinda hard to change.
title: About Me & This Site

contents: Loading…
title: Contact

contents:
Background Businesses like data. B2B businesses especially like to see the ins and outs of stock. And money doesn’t always go from client > business > supplier while goods go the other way. It can be reversed.
A reporting portal for the client and supplier is always a good way to manage that complexity.
The original reporting portal was a bit clunky from old code and outdated designs.
Objectives  Replicate original portal Allow clients to access supplier portals and vice versa Allow use of dynamic graphs Improve design of original portal  Main Tasks  Replicate reports from original portal Allow clients to hop across multiple databases to access previously inaccessible reports Allow easy creation of new graphs for visual display of data Improve security & reduce bugs Improve UI/UX  Approach  Language/Framework: Originally I tried to use Wordpress. It didn’t work too well thanks to the Microsoft SQL database (MSSQL), so I switched to Laravel. Since this was my first time using Laravel, I just installed the default TALL stack (Tailwind, AlpineJS, Livewire, Laravel) alongside Jetstream. For the graphs, I used ApexCharts. UI/UX: I ended up redesigning a few of the Jetstream components. A sidebar was added on the right, while the header was replaced with a job & item search (Livewire!). Apparently the search function was quite popular with our clients. Deployment: Hosted on the company’s IIS server, in a subfolder of the Wordpress installation. I had to rewrite the web.config file to get that working. Notes: Thanks to a lot of the default scaffolding, I didn’t need to write much authentication stuff despite much of the existing database not fitting with “the Laravel way”.  Results The managing director and the retired owner of the company was very pleased with the work.
title: Reporting Portal for Clients

contents:
Background I am a lazy person. Data-entry may have been in my job description, but I absolutely hate doing it. Why? It’s so freaking repetitive and it’s way too easy to mess up. When I switched to working from home, motivation to these boring repetitive tasks plummeted even more. And then there were even more time constraints after I decided to go back to study and I still had to work through a huge backlog of non-data-entry tasks.
For the sake of my mental health and to be more productive with my other more enjoyable (?) tasks, I decided to make this “Screw Accounting” project.
Objectives  Create a simplified, user-friendly way of automating particular data-entry tasks. Save time, save mental health, save my life.  Approach  Language/Framework: I was lookiing to do make this quickly - a simple app for a few automation tasks - using either of the languages I knew best at this time (PHP/Python). PHP has way too many dependencies. I didn’t need CRUD either. So I went with Python and made a Flask app. I did do some blueprint organisation so it would be easier to extend than the standard “dump everything to a handy app.py file”. Site UI/UX: Wasn’t too important since it was just me using it. Go Bootstrap! Deployment: Hosted on the WSL Ubuntu installation on my work laptop. It’s a virtual environment, which works pretty good since it’s just me using it.  Main Tasks  Connect to XeroAPI to send/receive data.  Remarketing Invoices: Get invoice data from company’s MSSQL database, create new invoice in XeroAPI and upload PDF attachment from company drive. Creditor invoices: Read their PDFs, aggressively extract needed information, create invoice & attach file with Xero API. Classify payroll into respective departments: Get payslips from payrun, lookup staff department, auto-dump this data to an Excel file, sum each department’s cost then create manual journal.   General data collection from XeroAPI  Extract raw data in formats that are unavailable directly from xero.com for further processing.   General Excel spreadsheet creation & automation  Results I saved about 5 hours of work per week. My mental health improved! Hooray!
But then I spent the time saved on the other parts of my job, which is great for the company, but not so much for my mental health. Plus had more classes. sCry!
title: Screw Accounting

contents:
Background When items get transferred within the warehouse, their location records in the database are updated so people know where to find ‘em. The full company app is simply too complicated to lug around the warehouse, so a slim version of it is used.
But even if it was slim? It was a pain to use. The slim version had sizes that transformed quite terribly onto tablets used for scanning. Buttons would disappear into the middle of nowhere. But you needed to click them to be “in” a location and see its items. Stuff was scrollable wasn’t really. It was also kinda slow and the last item would sometimes disappear into development hell, so you had to scan it twice.
For the sake of my colleagues mental health (?), I was told to build a light web app to fit this purpose.
Objectives My supervisor’s objectives  Optimise for mobile & other screens  My objectives  Laziness is king! I don’t wanna click a damn button! People must be just like me! (Probably not true) Reduce page refreshes (they clunky ugly) Look kinda good (I think my original designs were better but whatever my supervisor says) Ideally make it faster?  Main Tasks  Input a warehouse location + bring up items in location from database Have an input field to grab all scanned item codes Retrieve the info of the scanned item codes from database and make it appear inside the warehouse location Click a button to confirm transfer Message to confirm transfer Allow bulk transfer of items from one location to another location.  Approach  Language/Framework: The go-to language in this company remains to be PHP. The company wanted a lightweight app, so vanilla PHP was used alongside a simple .haccess-based router. The app uses the full version of jQuery/AJAX (mostly AJAX) which was partly installed alongside Bootstrap. UI/UX: The original app had a number of button clicks, after every time you filled in an input field. This one uses AJAX to fetch data without getting those and needing to tap so many buttons. Certain fields would only appear after others were filled as required by my supervisor. Made it so form elements so pressing enter/return in one input field would act as a tab to the next input field. Elements were mostly styled with the CDN version of bootstrap. Deployment: Hosted on the company’s Ubuntu server that was apparently originally for the company’s tech services’ internal help and documentation. I renamed the project folder /dg-warehouse-scan to /warehouse to make things easier on typing. Fortunately all this required was a quick adaptation of the .htaccess and the config files. Notes: Security was not an important priority as it was to be hosted on an internal website, but some fixes are still in place. Warnings were added to prevent major input-related issues, but they do not catch all. Also, the config details are in a folder below the served folder.  Results Definitely more optimised for smaller screens. It’s also faster than the original, at least according to my supervisor.
title: Warehouse Transfer App
